description:
You are an on-call operations engineer who has been paged about a spike of HTTP 5xx errors coming from one of your web-frontends on 12 Oct 2023 between 14:00 and 14:15 local time.  
Your goal is to isolate the relevant log lines and build a compact summary that your incident commander can paste directly into the incident chat.

Existing setup
1. A single text log file already exists at  
   /home/user/logs/combined.log  
   Every line in that file is a single JSON object followed by a newline (no pretty-printing, one record per line).  
   The exact format of every line is:  
      {"ts":"TIMESTAMP","level":"LEVEL","code":CODE,"msg":"TEXT"}  
   where  
   • TIMESTAMP is in RFC 3339 format (e.g. 2023-10-12T14:03:11Z)  
   • LEVEL is one of DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR  
   • CODE is an integer that identifies the error/warning class (e.g. 50203)  
   • TEXT is an arbitrary message that may contain spaces but never any quotation marks.  
   Example line:  
      {"ts":"2023-10-12T14:03:11Z","level":"ERROR","code":50203,"msg":"upstream connection reset"}

2. The incident commander needs two artefacts placed under /home/user/incident_triage (create the directory if it does not exist):

   a) /home/user/incident_triage/filtered_2023-10-12_1400-1415.log  
      • Contains only those lines from combined.log that satisfy ALL of the following:  
        - ts is on 2023-10-12  
        - ts is greater than or equal to 14:00:00Z and strictly less than 14:15:00Z  
        - level is either WARN or ERROR  
      • Lines must appear in the original chronological order.  
      • The file must end with a single newline character (typical Unix text file).

   b) /home/user/incident_triage/error_summary.txt  
      • A two-column, whitespace-separated report summarising the contents of filtered_2023-10-12_1400-1415.log.  
      • For every unique CODE that appeared in the filtered log, output exactly one line of the form  
            CODE COUNT  
        where CODE is the integer error/warning code and COUNT is the number of times that code occurs in the filtered file.  
      • Lines must be sorted by CODE in ascending numerical order.  
      • No header line, no commas, no extra spaces — just CODE, a single space, COUNT, and a trailing newline.

Constraints & hints
• You only need standard GNU/Linux userland tools likely already available in an apptainer image (grep, awk, sed, cut, sort, uniq, etc.).  
• Expect the solution to require roughly 5–10 commands total (the evaluation script will not check the command history, only the resulting files).  
• Do not modify /home/user/logs/combined.log itself.  
• Feel free to create any temporary files in /home/user/incident_triage but the verification will ignore them.  
• Avoid hard-coding absolute line numbers — use proper regular expressions and/or JSON-aware tools (e.g. grep -E, awk) so that the solution would still work if more lines were appended to combined.log.

Success criteria
The automated test will mark the task as passed only if BOTH of the following conditions are met:

1. /home/user/incident_triage/filtered_2023-10-12_1400-1415.log exists and its content matches exactly the subset of log lines fulfilling the three filters (date, time range, level ∈ {WARN,ERROR}).

2. /home/user/incident_triage/error_summary.txt exists and its content is exactly the correct list of “CODE COUNT” pairs, one per line, sorted numerically by CODE, with nothing else in the file.

No other output artefacts are graded, but command stdout/stderr is fine.
